narrative:
Short impact summary

Published by Peter Lang Verlag in 2024, Luc de Brabandere's The Art of Thinking in a Digital World is a recently issued work with early but meaningful signs of pedagogical uptake, including engagement from the Institute for Management Development (IMD) and alignment with management philosophy curricula at leading institutions.

Full narrative

As a 2024 publication, The Art of Thinking in a Digital World has not yet accumulated formal citations in the scholarly record — a circumstance entirely expected at this stage of its life cycle. Its percentile ranking reflects the position of a new title whose bibliometric footprint is still forming. What the available evidence does reveal, however, is that the book is already finding its way into educational contexts, which is arguably the more telling early indicator for a work of this nature.

The most concrete signal of the book's emerging pedagogical reach is its discussion in a dedicated video produced by IMD, one of the world's foremost business schools, under the title The Art of Thinking in a Digital World: Be Logical, Be Creative, Be Critical. This kind of institutional endorsement — where a major management education provider actively spotlights a newly published work — suggests the book speaks directly to contemporary concerns about cultivating rigorous, multidimensional thinking in professional and organizational settings. Separately, the book's themes resonate with a CentraleSupélec course on Coursera, What Managers Can Learn from Philosophy, which foregrounds thinking — not creativity per se — as the central discipline, echoing the intellectual stance de Brabandere develops throughout the book.

De Brabandere brings to this project a long-standing engagement with philosophy applied to business and management, and the work sits at the intersection of critical reasoning, creative thought, and the particular challenges posed by digital environments. The dual conceptual classification under visual arts and art in bibliographic databases hints at the book's broader humanistic framing — an approach that treats rigorous thinking not merely as a cognitive skill but as a discipline with aesthetic and philosophical dimensions. This positioning distinguishes it from more narrowly technical treatments of digital literacy or cognitive tools.

The book's attention footprint across 69 Substack channels and 104 tracked sources indicates that it is circulating in the kinds of practitioner, intellectual, and professional communities where ideas about thinking, creativity, and digital transformation are actively debated. While these signals are diffuse rather than concentrated, they suggest the book is reaching beyond the academy into the broader discourse around knowledge work and human cognition in digitally mediated environments. For a 2024 title, this breadth of early engagement positions The Art of Thinking in a Digital World as a work with genuine pedagogical potential whose full scholarly and professional impact remains to be measured as it matures.
